meta.title: MS-PBI-1: Power Query, DAX, Modeling and Report Deployment

questions.stem: You connect to a SQL Server database in Power Query. The query retrieves 2 million rows but only 100,000 are needed after filtering. You want to reduce data transfer and improve refresh performance. What should you do?

options.text: Apply a filter step in Power Query after importing all rows, then fold the query back to SQL
Enable query folding by applying the filter step immediately after the source step so the filter is pushed to SQL Server
Import all rows and use a calculated column with IF to exclude unnecessary rows
Use DirectQuery mode instead of Import so no data is transferred

questions.explanation:
Query folding translates Power Query transformation steps into native SQL query operations executed on the source database. Applying the filter step directly after the Source step maximizes the chance of folding, pushing the WHERE clause to SQL Server and reducing data transfer. You can verify folding via the 'View Native Query' option. Applying filters after non-foldable steps breaks the fold chain.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: A Power Query table has a column named [Sales Amount] that contains text values like '$1,234.56'. You need to convert it to a decimal number. What is the correct sequence of transformations?

options.text: Change the column data type directly to Decimal Number
Use Replace Values to remove '$' and ',', then change data type to Decimal Number
Add a custom column using Number.FromText([Sales Amount])
Use Text.ToNumber in a new custom column

questions.explanation:
Directly changing a text column with currency symbols and thousand separators to Decimal Number causes conversion errors. The correct approach is to first clean the text: Replace Values to remove '$', then Replace Values to remove ',', and finally change the data type to Decimal Number (or use locale-aware type change with locale set to English US).

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: You have two queries: 'Orders' and 'Returns'. You need to combine them to see all orders with a flag indicating whether each was returned. Some orders have no corresponding return record. Which Power Query merge join type should you use?

options.text: Inner join
Left outer join
Full outer join
Right anti join

questions.explanation:
A Left outer join keeps all rows from the left table (Orders) and brings in matching rows from the right table (Returns). Orders with no return match get null values in the Returns columns, which can be used to flag unmatched orders. Inner join would exclude orders without returns. Full outer join would also include returns with no matching order, which is not needed here.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: You want to create a date dimension table in Power BI that covers the range of dates found in your fact table's [Order Date] column automatically. Which approach generates this table without hardcoding date ranges?

options.text: Mark the built-in auto date/time table as the date table
Create a calculated table using CALENDARAUTO() in DAX
Import a CSV with predefined dates from a static file
Use a Power Query M function with List.Dates and hardcoded start/end dates

questions.explanation:
CALENDARAUTO() scans all date columns in the data model and automatically generates a contiguous date table from the earliest to the latest date found, with a one-row-per-day granularity. This ensures the date table always covers the fact table's date range as data grows. CALENDARAUTO() is the DAX best practice for dynamic date table generation.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: A Power Query refresh fails with 'Formula.Firewall: Query references other queries or steps and so may not directly access a data source.' What is the cause and fix?

options.text: The query references a step in a different query; merge the steps into a single query
Privacy levels are not set; two data sources with different privacy levels are being combined without explicit permission
The Power Query engine cannot process more than one data source per query
The model has circular references between queries; remove the circular dependency

questions.explanation:
The Formula Firewall prevents Power Query from combining data from sources with different privacy levels (Public, Organizational, Private) unless explicitly configured. This protects against data leakage. The fix is to set privacy levels on data sources in File > Options > Privacy, or in the on-premises data gateway settings. Setting both sources to the same privacy level or configuring data privacy to 'Always ignore' (for trusted environments) resolves the error.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: In a Power BI data model, the relationship between a Dates table and a Sales fact table is a one-to-many relationship with a single-directional cross-filter. An analyst reports that a slicer on the Dates table does not filter a separate Products dimension table. What should you do?

options.text: Change the Dates-to-Sales relationship to bidirectional cross-filter
Create a direct relationship between the Dates table and the Products table
Enable bidirectional cross-filter on the Sales-to-Products relationship
Add Products as a role-playing dimension connected to Sales

questions.explanation:
In a standard star schema, filters propagate from dimension tables to fact tables (one-to-many, single direction). For a Dates slicer to filter Products, the filter must travel: Dates → Sales → Products. This requires the Sales-to-Products relationship to allow bidirectional cross-filter, or you use CROSSFILTER in DAX. Enabling bidirectional on Dates-to-Sales would filter fact data both ways (risk of incorrect aggregations). The correct fix is bidirectional on Sales→Products or a DAX measure with CROSSFILTER.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You need to calculate year-to-date (YTD) total sales in a DAX measure. The fiscal year ends on June 30. Which DAX function should you use?

options.text: TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date])
TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date], "06-30")
CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date], "06/30"))
Both B and C are correct

questions.explanation:
Both TOTALYTD with the year_end_date parameter and CALCULATE with DATESYTD and the year_end_date parameter produce the same result for fiscal year YTD. The year_end_date argument takes a string in MM-DD or MM/DD format. TOTALYTD([Total Sales], Dates[Date], "06-30") and CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date], "06/30")) are functionally equivalent.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: A data model contains a Sales fact table and a Customers dimension. You need a measure that calculates the average sales per customer, counting only customers who made at least one purchase in the filtered context. Which DAX expression is correct?

options.text: AVERAGE(Sales[Amount])
DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), COUNTROWS(Customers))
DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[CustomerID]))
AVERAGEX(Customers, SUM(Sales[Amount]))

questions.explanation:
DIVIDE(SUM(Sales[Amount]), DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[CustomerID])) calculates total sales divided by the count of distinct customers who appear in the Sales table within the filter context — i.e., only customers who made purchases. COUNTROWS(Customers) would count all customers in the Customers table regardless of whether they purchased, which inflates the denominator. AVERAGE(Sales[Amount]) averages by row, not by customer.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: A measure [Sales LY] uses CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date])). When the report is filtered to a single month (March 2024), [Sales LY] returns March 2023 correctly. But when filtered to a custom date range (Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2024), [Sales LY] returns an incorrect value. Why?

options.text: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR requires a contiguous date range; partial month ranges are not supported
SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR shifts any set of dates back by one year; it works correctly — the user's expectation is wrong
The Dates table is missing a row for Feb 15, 2023, causing the calculation to return null
SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR only works with standard calendar periods (month, quarter, year); use DATEADD(Dates[Date], -1, YEAR) for custom ranges

questions.explanation:
SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR returns the same set of dates shifted back exactly one year. For Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2024, it returns Jan 1 – Feb 15, 2023. This is mathematically correct. If the user expects something different (e.g., full year or specific business period), SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR is working as designed. The 'incorrect value' is the user's expectation issue. SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR works on any set of dates, not just full periods.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You have a single Sales table and need to create measures for 'Sales to New Customers' and 'Sales to Returning Customers'. A new customer is one whose first purchase was in the current filter context. Which DAX technique is most appropriate?

options.text: Use two separate calculated columns: IsNewCustomer and IsReturningCustomer, then SUM them
Use CALCULATE with FILTER and MINX to find customers whose minimum order date equals a date within the current period
Create a customer segmentation calculated table and relate it to Sales
Use the built-in New vs Returning customer quick measure

questions.explanation:
The DAX pattern for new customer sales uses CALCULATE to evaluate sales for customers whose earliest purchase (MINX or CALCULATE(MIN(Sales[Date]), ALLEXCEPT(Sales, Sales[CustomerID]))) falls within the current filter context period. This is a FILTER-over-VALUES pattern. Calculated columns compute row-by-row at load time and cannot respond dynamically to filter context. Power BI Quick Measures provide a simpler UI but the underlying logic is the same CALCULATE/FILTER pattern.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You create a relationship between FactSales[ProductID] (many side) and DimProduct[ProductID] (one side). The relationship is valid in Power BI. What must be true about DimProduct[ProductID]?

options.text: It must be the primary key of DimProduct with no duplicate or blank values
It must be an integer data type
It must match every ProductID value found in FactSales
It must be set as the table's key column in the model view

questions.explanation:
The 'one' side of a one-to-many relationship in Power BI must have unique, non-null values. Power BI enforces this constraint when creating relationships — if DimProduct[ProductID] contains duplicates or blanks, the relationship cannot be created as a 1:many relationship (it would become a many:many). Data type does not need to be integer; any comparable type works.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/transform-model/

questions.stem: A measure returns different results when placed in a table visual versus a card visual. In the table it shows per-product values correctly; in the card (no row context) it returns blank. The measure is: Sales Rank = RANKX(ALL(Products[ProductName]), [Total Sales]). What is the issue?

options.text: RANKX requires an explicit column in the first argument; use ALLSELECTED instead of ALL
In the card visual there is no row filter context, so RANKX ranks all products and returns 1 for the grand total — the measure is working correctly
The card visual does not support rank measures; use a table visual instead
Add ISINSCOPE(Products[ProductName]) to return blank at the grand total level intentionally

questions.explanation:
RANKX over ALL(Products[ProductName]) with no row context (card visual) evaluates [Total Sales] for every product and ranks the grand total among them — typically returning 1. If the intent is to show rank only at the product level (not grand total), wrapping the measure with IF(ISINSCOPE(Products[ProductName]), RANKX(...), BLANK()) suppresses the misleading grand total rank. This is a common rank measure pattern.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: A report page has a slicer for [Year] and a line chart showing monthly sales. You want the chart to show all years' data as a comparison regardless of the Year slicer selection. What should you do?

options.text: Set the visual-level filter on the line chart to exclude the Year field
Use Edit interactions from the slicer to set the interaction with the line chart to 'None'
Create a measure using ALL(Dates[Year]) to remove the year filter context
Duplicate the page and remove the slicer from the duplicate

questions.explanation:
Power BI's Edit Interactions feature allows you to control whether a slicer or visual filters another visual. Setting the interaction from the Year slicer to the line chart to 'None' (the crossed-out icon) prevents the slicer from filtering that visual. This is a report-level configuration requiring no code changes. Edit Interactions is accessed by selecting the slicer and clicking Format > Edit interactions.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You need to display sales by country on a map visual. Countries are stored as two-letter ISO codes (e.g., 'US', 'GB'). The map shows incorrect locations for some countries. What should you do?

options.text: Replace ISO codes with full country names
Set the Data Category of the country column to 'Country or Region' in the column properties
Use a filled map instead of a bubble map
Enable the 'Use Microsoft Bing geocoding' option in report settings

questions.explanation:
Power BI uses data category metadata to interpret geographic data correctly. Setting the Data Category to 'Country or Region' (via Column Tools tab in Power BI Desktop) helps the map visual and Bing geocoder understand that the column contains country identifiers. Without this, Power BI may misinterpret 'US' as a US state abbreviation or city name. This is the first step before further geocoding troubleshooting.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/create-reports/

questions.stem: A bar chart shows top 10 products by sales. You want to add a reference line showing the average sales across all products regardless of the top 10 filter. Which approach achieves this?

options.text: Add an analytics pane average line using 'Average' from the Analytics panel
Create a measure: AVG All Products = CALCULATE(AVERAGE(Sales[Amount]), ALL(Products)) and add it to the visual
Use a constant line from the Analytics pane with a hardcoded average value
Both A and B are valid approaches

questions.explanation:
The Analytics pane average line computes the average within the current visual filter context (top 10 products only), not across all products. To show the average across ALL products regardless of the top-N filter, a DAX measure using CALCULATE with ALL(Products) removes the product filter context. This is then plotted as a separate measure series or added as a constant line with a dynamic value. option **A** would show the average of the top-10 only.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You want report consumers to be able to drill through from a summary sales page to a detailed transactions page filtered by the selected product. How do you configure this?

options.text: Create a bookmark for the detail page and link it from the summary visual
Add the Product field to the Drill through well of the detail page in the Visualizations pane
Enable Page Navigation in the summary visual's Format pane
Use the Sync slicers feature to synchronize the product slicer across pages

questions.explanation:
Power BI drill-through is configured on the destination page by adding the field to the Drill through well in the Visualizations pane. When a user right-clicks a visual on the source page and selects 'Drill through > [detail page name]', Power BI navigates to the detail page with the selected field value as the filter context. This is Power BI's native drill-through mechanism.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/create-reports/

questions.stem: A report uses a measure [Profit Margin %] = DIVIDE([Profit], [Revenue]). When added to a matrix with subtotals, the subtotal rows show incorrect margin percentages (they appear to be averaging the row percentages). What is the root cause?

options.text: DIVIDE averages its inputs at aggregation levels by default; use SUM instead
Matrix subtotals apply SUM aggregation to the measure values of child rows instead of re-evaluating the measure in the subtotal's filter context
The measure is working correctly; the user misunderstands margin aggregation
Use SUMX over the matrix rows to produce correct subtotals

questions.explanation:
When a matrix shows subtotals, Power BI re-evaluates the DAX measure at the subtotal's filter context (the combined filter of the subtotal group). DIVIDE([Profit], [Revenue]) at the subtotal level correctly sums all profit and revenue in that group then divides — it does NOT average child row percentages. If subtotals look wrong, the issue is often that Profit or Revenue measures have incorrect aggregation at the subtotal level (e.g., using AVERAGE instead of SUM). The measure itself is not averaging row percentages; DAX always re-evaluates in filter context.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You need to implement row-level security (RLS) so that each regional manager can only see data for their own region. The mapping of managers to regions is in a separate table 'ManagerRegions'. What is the correct setup?

options.text: Create an RLS role with a DAX filter: [Region] = USERPRINCIPALNAME()
Create an RLS role that filters the ManagerRegions table: [ManagerEmail] = USERPRINCIPALNAME(), ensure ManagerRegions relates to the Region dimension
Create a calculated column in the fact table using USERPRINCIPALNAME() and filter on it
Use Object Level Security to hide the Region column from non-admin users

questions.explanation:
Dynamic RLS using USERPRINCIPALNAME() should be applied on the ManagerRegions mapping table, which relates to the Region dimension. The DAX filter [ManagerEmail] = USERPRINCIPALNAME() restricts the ManagerRegions table to the logged-in user's row(s), which cascades through the relationship to filter the Region dimension and ultimately the fact table. Applying USERPRINCIPALNAME() directly on the Region column only works if regions are named with email addresses, which is not the case here.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: A Q&A visual is not recognizing the synonym 'revenue' for the measure named 'Total Sales'. How do you fix this?

options.text: Rename the measure to 'Revenue'
Add 'revenue' as a synonym in the Q&A linguistic schema / Synonyms feature in the model
Create a second measure named 'Revenue' that references [Total Sales]
Enable the Q&A natural language training feature in Power BI service settings

questions.explanation:
Power BI Q&A supports custom synonyms defined in the Synonyms pane (accessible via the model view or via the Q&A setup in the report). Adding 'revenue' as a synonym for the [Total Sales] measure allows Q&A to recognize user queries using either term. The linguistic schema XML file can also be edited directly for advanced customization. Renaming the measure would break existing report references.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You want to highlight values in a table visual where sales are below target. Sales above or equal to target should be green; below target should be red. Which feature should you use?

options.text: Create a conditional formatting rule using background color with rules-based formatting on the sales column
Add a KPI visual next to the table
Create a calculated column returning 'Green' or 'Red' and add it to the table
Use a data bar with a positive/negative threshold

questions.explanation:
Power BI's conditional formatting for table and matrix visuals allows rules-based background or font color formatting. You configure rules like 'If value < [Target] then Red else Green'. This is available in the Format pane under 'Conditional formatting'. It dynamically responds to filter context and does not require additional columns. Calculated columns would not respond to filter context changes.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/transform-model/desktop-calculated-columns

questions.stem: You have a Power BI report in a Premium workspace. You want to give external users from a partner organization read-only access to the report without creating Microsoft 365 accounts for them. What feature enables this?

options.text: Power BI guest access via Azure AD B2B
Publish to web (public URL)
Embed the report in a SharePoint Online page
Export to PDF and email

questions.explanation:
Azure AD B2B (Business-to-Business) allows external users to access Power BI content using their own organizational identity. Administrators invite external users via Azure AD, and they access the report with read permissions without needing a Microsoft 365 account in your tenant. Publish to web creates a publicly accessible, unauthenticated URL — not appropriate for partner access with access control. SharePoint embedding requires Microsoft 365 accounts.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/enterprise/

questions.stem: A dataset refreshes from an on-premises SQL Server database. The refresh fails with 'Unable to connect to the data source'. The on-premises data gateway is installed and running. What should you check first?

options.text: Verify that the gateway machine has outbound access to the Power BI service endpoints on port 443
Reinstall Power BI Desktop to clear gateway credentials
Switch the dataset from Import to DirectQuery mode
Upgrade to a Premium capacity to enable on-premises refresh

questions.explanation:
The on-premises data gateway communicates with Power BI service via outbound HTTPS (port 443) and optionally Azure Service Bus (port 5671/5672). If the gateway is running but refresh fails, the most common cause is a firewall blocking outbound connectivity from the gateway machine to Power BI service endpoints. Checking network/firewall rules is the first diagnostic step. Premium capacity is not required for on-premises data gateway refresh.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/connect-data/desktop-directquery-about

questions.stem: You use a deployment pipeline (Dev → Test → Production) in Power BI. After deploying to Production, you discover that dataset connection strings still point to the Test database. What should you configure to fix this for future deployments?

options.text: Manually update connection strings after each deployment in Production
Configure deployment pipeline rules to map parameters or data source connection details between stages
Create separate PBIX files for each environment
Use Power Query parameters set to the Test environment values in all stages

questions.explanation:
Power BI deployment pipelines support deployment rules that automatically transform dataset properties (data source connection strings, Power Query parameter values) when deploying between stages. Rules are configured per pipeline stage and apply during each deployment, eliminating manual post-deployment updates. This is the purpose-built feature for environment-specific configuration in deployment pipelines.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: A large Power BI dataset (import mode, 20 GB) refreshes daily and the full refresh takes 4 hours. Business users need data refreshed every 30 minutes for recent transactions. Which strategy reduces refresh time while keeping historical data current?

options.text: Switch the entire dataset to DirectQuery
Enable incremental refresh with a 'detect data changes' column to refresh only the current day's partition and keep historical partitions frozen
Split into two datasets: historical (daily refresh) and current-day (DirectQuery)
Reduce the dataset size by removing historical data older than 1 year

questions.explanation:
Power BI incremental refresh splits the dataset into date partitions. Historical partitions are frozen after the incremental period and are never re-refreshed. Only the current partition (e.g., today) is refreshed on each cycle, reducing refresh time from hours to minutes. The 'detect data changes' option uses a watermark column to skip refreshes if source data hasn't changed. This enables sub-hourly refresh for recent data without full refresh overhead.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/connect-data/desktop-directquery-about

questions.stem: You want to certify a dataset in your organization so that report authors know it is the trusted, authoritative source. Which Power BI feature enables this?

options.text: Set the dataset as 'Featured' in the workspace
Apply the 'Certified' endorsement to the dataset
Mark the dataset as 'Official' in Power BI Desktop
Add a 'Certified' tag using workspace metadata settings

questions.explanation:
Power BI supports dataset endorsement with two levels: 'Promoted' (self-service, set by the dataset owner) and 'Certified' (set by administrators or designated certifiers after quality review). Certified datasets display a certification badge in the data hub and dataset search results, signaling to report authors that this is the trusted source. Certification is configured in the dataset settings in Power BI service.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/enterprise/

questions.stem: You have a many-to-many relationship between Products and Promotions (a product can have multiple promotions; a promotion can apply to multiple products). How should you model this in Power BI?

options.text: Create a direct many-to-many relationship between Products and Promotions tables
Create a bridge table ProductPromotions with ProductID and PromotionID, create one-to-many relationships from Products and Promotions to the bridge table
Duplicate the Promotions table and create two one-to-many relationships
Use a calculated table with CROSSJOIN to generate all combinations

questions.explanation:
Many-to-many relationships are best handled with a bridge (junction) table. Creating ProductPromotions with one row per product-promotion combination, then one-to-many relationships from Products → ProductPromotions and Promotions → ProductPromotions creates a proper star schema. Power BI does support native many-to-many relationships (set cardinality to Many-to-Many), but the bridge table approach is preferred for explicit control over aggregation semantics and better performance.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/transform-model/

questions.stem: You need to combine data from 12 monthly Excel files in a folder into a single table. Each file has the same schema. Which Power Query approach is most maintainable as new monthly files are added?

options.text: Manually import each file and append the 12 queries
Use 'Get Data > Folder', combine files using the combine function, which automatically processes all files in the folder
Use a Power Query loop to iterate over file names
Create an ODBC connection to the folder

questions.explanation:
The Power Query folder connector (Get Data > Folder) lists all files in a directory and applies a combine function to merge them. When new monthly files are added to the folder, refreshing the query automatically includes the new files — no query changes needed. Manual appending requires adding a new query each month. Power Query has no loop construct.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: A report page has a bar chart and a table visual. You want to configure the bar chart so that clicking a bar filters the table, but the table does not filter the bar chart when a row is selected. How do you configure this?

options.text: Select the table, go to Format > Edit Interactions, and set the bar chart interaction to None
Select the bar chart, go to Format > Edit Interactions, and set the table interaction to Filter
Select the bar chart, go to Format > Edit Interactions, set table to Filter; then select the table and set the bar chart to None
Disable cross-filtering in the report settings

questions.explanation:
Edit Interactions is set FROM the source visual TO each target. To make the bar chart filter the table: select bar chart → set table to Filter (default). To prevent the table from filtering the bar chart: select table → set bar chart to None. Both interactions need to be configured independently. option **B** only configures one direction.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/create-reports/

questions.stem: A CALCULATE expression uses REMOVEFILTERS(DimDate[Year]) as a filter modifier. What does REMOVEFILTERS do in this context compared to ALL(DimDate[Year])?

options.text: REMOVEFILTERS and ALL(DimDate[Year]) are functionally identical as filter modifiers in CALCULATE
REMOVEFILTERS removes only external filters; ALL removes all filters including those from relationships
REMOVEFILTERS is a newer alias for ALL when used as a CALCULATE modifier; they are semantically equivalent in this context
REMOVEFILTERS cannot be used with a column argument; it only accepts table arguments

questions.explanation:
REMOVEFILTERS was introduced as a semantic alias for ALL when used as a CALCULATE filter modifier. REMOVEFILTERS(DimDate[Year]) and ALL(DimDate[Year]) in CALCULATE produce identical results: both remove any active filter on DimDate[Year] from the filter context. REMOVEFILTERS was introduced to make code intent clearer (removing filters vs. returning all values), but they are functionally equivalent as CALCULATE modifiers.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You need to unpivot a table where months are column headers (Jan, Feb, Mar...) and product names are row headers. The result should have columns: Product, Month, Sales. Which Power Query transformation achieves this?

options.text: Select the Product column, then choose 'Unpivot Other Columns'
Select all month columns, then choose 'Unpivot Columns'
Both A and B produce the same result
Use 'Transpose' followed by 'Unpivot'

questions.explanation:
In Power Query, both approaches produce the same result. 'Unpivot Columns' (selecting month columns and unpivoting) explicitly unpivots selected columns. 'Unpivot Other Columns' (selecting Product and choosing 'Unpivot Other Columns') unpivots everything except the selected column. Both are equivalent and generate the same M code logic. 'Unpivot Other Columns' is more robust when column names change (new months are added).

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: You need to prevent report authors from connecting to a dataset in a workspace unless their workspace is in the same Premium capacity. Which setting enforces this?

options.text: Enable 'Block republish and disable package refresh' in workspace settings
Restrict cross-workspace access using 'Discourage live connections from reports on other workspaces' in dataset settings
Remove Build permission from all users for the dataset
Enable XMLA endpoint restriction to Premium-only connections

questions.explanation:
Power BI dataset settings include 'Discourage live connections from reports on other workspaces', which prevents report authors in other workspaces from creating live connections to the dataset. This enforces colocation of reports and datasets. Removing Build permission entirely would prevent all external connections including legitimate ones. XMLA endpoint restriction is for third-party tool access.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/enterprise/

questions.stem: You need to show the percentage contribution of each product to total sales in a bar chart, where the total always represents 100% of currently filtered data. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(Products)))
Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLSELECTED(Products)))
Change the bar chart Y-axis to percentage format
Use DIVIDE([Product Sales], SUM(Sales[Amount]))

questions.explanation:
ALLSELECTED(Products) removes filters from the Products table while preserving any external slicer/filter context. This means the denominator reflects the total sales of all currently visible products (respecting slicers outside the visual) rather than all products in the dataset. ALL(Products) would always sum all products regardless of slicers. DIVIDE([Product Sales], SUM(Sales[Amount])) in a per-product context returns 100% for every row since SUM(Sales[Amount]) equals [Product Sales] at that granularity.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: Which Power BI storage mode allows data to be cached in the Power BI service for fast query performance while also allowing live queries to the source for the most recent data?

options.text: Import mode
DirectQuery mode
Dual (Mixed) mode
Live connection to Analysis Services

questions.explanation:
Dual mode (also called Mixed or Dual storage mode) allows a table to behave as either Import or DirectQuery depending on the query context. When used with dimension tables alongside DirectQuery fact tables, the engine queries cached data for dimensions (fast) and live data for facts (current). This hybrid approach gives better performance than pure DirectQuery while maintaining freshness for large fact tables.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/connect-data/desktop-directquery-about

questions.stem: A Power Query step transforms a column using a formula that references a column that was renamed in a later step. The query throws an error. What is the root cause?

options.text: Power Query steps are evaluated top-to-bottom; the column is renamed in a later step, so at the time of the formula step the original name is valid — renaming later causes downstream steps to fail
Power Query does not support renaming columns that are referenced in formulas
The formula references a column that was renamed, so it cannot find the original name
Power Query formulas must use column index positions, not names

questions.explanation:
Power Query evaluates each step sequentially. When a column is renamed in step N, any step before N still uses the original name (which is correct). However, if a later step was written referencing the original name and the rename occurs before that step, the reference becomes invalid. Most commonly, inserting a rename step before an existing formula step that uses the old name breaks the formula. The column name in the formula must match the name at that point in the sequence.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: Your organization wants to automate Power BI dataset refresh using the REST API from an Azure Data Factory pipeline after the ETL loads data. Which authentication method is recommended for service-to-service API calls?

options.text: Service principal with the dataset's workspace role assignment and Power BI Admin API permission
User account credentials stored in Azure Key Vault
Master user account with Power BI Pro license using username/password OAuth flow
API key generated in Power BI tenant settings

questions.explanation:
Service principals (Azure AD application registrations) are the recommended authentication method for service-to-service Power BI REST API automation. They do not require a licensed user account, support certificate or secret authentication, and permissions are granted via workspace roles (Member or Admin). Service principals must be enabled in Power BI tenant settings (Allow service principals to use Power BI APIs). Master user accounts with passwords are fragile and a security risk.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/enterprise/

questions.stem: A report uses bookmarks to switch between two chart types (bar and line) showing the same data. When the user selects a region from a slicer and then switches bookmarks, the slicer selection is lost. Why?

options.text: Bookmarks capture visual state including data filters by default; the slicer state was captured at bookmark creation time
Slicers are not supported in bookmark-based navigation
The bookmark was created without capturing slicer state; enable 'Data' in bookmark properties
Cross-page filtering is not preserved by bookmarks

questions.explanation:
Power BI bookmarks capture the state of the report at the time they are created, including data filters (slicer selections), display state (which visuals are visible), and spotlight. When a bookmark is applied, it restores the exact state it captured — overwriting the user's current slicer selection. To preserve user slicer state when applying bookmarks for chart-type toggling, uncheck 'Data' in the bookmark's properties so only the visual visibility is changed, not the data filter context.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/create-reports/

questions.stem: A measure [Running Total] = CALCULATE([Total Sales], FILTER(ALL(Dates[Date]), Dates[Date] <= MAX(Dates[Date]))) works correctly in a table sorted by date. However, in a matrix with months as columns, it shows the same value (grand total) in all cells. What is the issue?

options.text: The matrix column context does not generate a row context; use EARLIER() to fix this
MAX(Dates[Date]) in a matrix column context returns the max date for that column's month; the FILTER should use LASTDATE(Dates[Date]) instead
ALL(Dates[Date]) removes the matrix column filter, and MAX(Dates[Date]) within FILTER evaluates to the overall max date; use ALLEXCEPT or ensure the filter context from columns is preserved
Running totals are only supported in table and line chart visuals, not matrices

questions.explanation:
ALL(Dates[Date]) removes all filters on Dates[Date], including the matrix column filter. MAX(Dates[Date]) then evaluates over all dates (returning the overall maximum), so every column shows the same grand total running sum. The fix is to either use a column filter that doesn't remove the matrix context, or restructure as CALCULATE([Total Sales], DATESYTD(Dates[Date])) for YTD, or use ALLSELECTED to preserve external filters while removing the visual's internal column filter.

https://learn.microsoft.com/dax/dax-overview

questions.stem: You need to add a column in Power Query that extracts the domain name from an email address column (e.g., 'user@company.com' → 'company.com'). Which M expression achieves this?

options.text: Text.AfterDelimiter([Email], "@")
Text.Split([Email], "@"){1}
Text.Middle([Email], Text.PositionOf([Email], "@") + 1)
All of the above produce the same result

questions.explanation:
All three M expressions extract the text after the '@' character. Text.AfterDelimiter is the cleanest and most readable approach. Text.Split returns a list split on '@'; index {1} gets the second element (the domain). Text.Middle with Text.PositionOf calculates the position of '@' and takes everything after it. All three produce 'company.com' from 'user@company.com'.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-query/power-query-what-is-power-query

questions.stem: A workspace contains reports used by 500 users. You need to ensure the workspace can handle concurrent query load without throttling. Which Power BI license/capacity model is required?

options.text: 500 individual Power BI Pro licenses
Power BI Premium Per Capacity (P-SKU) assigned to the workspace
Power BI Premium Per User (PPU) licenses for all 500 users
Power BI Embedded (A-SKU) for API-based distribution

questions.explanation:
Power BI Premium Per Capacity (P-SKU) assigns dedicated compute capacity to a workspace, providing isolated resources that scale with the SKU size. This handles high concurrent query loads without throttling shared Pro capacity resources. Premium Per User (PPU) provides Premium features per user but runs on shared infrastructure, not dedicated capacity. For 500 users with concurrent access and performance SLAs, Premium Per Capacity is the appropriate model.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/enterprise/

questions.stem: Which Power BI visual type is best for showing the distribution of a continuous variable (such as order amounts) and identifying skewness or outliers?

options.text: Clustered bar chart
Histogram or box plot visual
Donut chart
Funnel chart

questions.explanation:
A histogram shows the frequency distribution of a continuous variable across buckets, making it easy to identify the shape, central tendency, and spread. A box plot (available as a custom or built-in visual in newer Power BI versions) shows quartiles, median, and outliers directly. Both are purpose-built for distribution analysis. Clustered bar charts compare discrete categories; donut charts show part-to-whole for a small number of categories.

https://learn.microsoft.com/power-bi/create-reports/
